aliases: Truffle Mushroom with Parmesan and Butter

english: Truffle Mushroom with Parmesan and Butter

ontology.foodon_gap_note: No exact FoodOn entry for truffle and mushroom medley with cream and cheese; closest defensible anchor is mushroom food product (FOODON_03301710).
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 A00GJ covers cooked mushrooms and fungi; truffle-cream-cheese preparation is not separately coded. Approximate match used and documented.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No suitable SNOMED CT food concept identified for this composite dish.

serving.natural_language_portion: A typical restaurant serving of truffle and mushroom medley is approximately 150 g, presented as a side dish or starter alongside pasta, risotto, or grilled proteins.

nutrient_source.title: Truffle & Mushrooms — Singapore HPB Foodo nutrient record (cr_id G00010963)
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Nutrient values sourced directly from the HPB Foodo canonical record for this dish (cr_id G00010963, confidence 0.95). Secondary nutrient fields not reported by HPB (saturated fat, sugar, dietary fibre, MUFA, PUFA, trans fat) are estimated from USDA FoodData Central component data for cooked mixed mushrooms with butter and parmesan (USDA SR Legacy entries 11260, 01001, 01032) and documented accordingly.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2021 — mushrooms (cooked, mixed varieties)
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Atkinson FS et al., Diabetes Care 2021
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for truffle and mushroom medley with cream and cheese. GI estimated at 15 (low) based on cooked mixed mushrooms from the University of Sydney GI Database (Atkinson et al., Diabetes Care 2021), which report GI values of 10–20 for cooked mushroom preparations. The added fat (butter, truffle oil, cream) further suppresses glycaemic response. GL per 150 g serving = (15 × 6.3 × 1.5) / 100 = 1.4 g.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium 807 mg per 100 g — substantially exceeds the 600 mg/100 g upper threshold for hypertension-safe foods
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should avoid or strictly limit this dish; sodium at 807 mg/100 g is driven by parmigiano reggiano, black truffle cream, and added salt, and a 150 g serving delivers approximately 1,211 mg sodium — more than half the 2,000 mg daily limit.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Low carbohydrate (6.3 g/100 g) and low estimated GI (~15) are favourable, but high total fat (28.5 g/100 g) and saturated fat (~9.8 g/100 g) may impair insulin sensitivity
type2_diabetes.guidance: The low carbohydrate content and low GI make this dish glycaemically safe in moderate portions, but the high saturated fat load from butter and cheese should be considered as chronic high saturated fat intake is associated with reduced insulin sensitivity in type 2 diabetes management.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: High sodium (807 mg/100 g), elevated saturated fat (~9.8 g/100 g), and cholesterol (105 mg/100 g) collectively raise cardiovascular risk
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: This dish presents multiple cardiovascular risk factors simultaneously — high sodium elevates blood pressure, saturated fat from butter and cheese raises LDL cholesterol, and dietary cholesterol at 105 mg/100 g adds further burden; individuals with cardiovascular disease should consume this dish only occasionally and in small portions.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Mushroom beta-glucans offer immune-modulating benefit, but high sodium (807 mg/100 g) and saturated fat (~9.8 g/100 g) promote pro-inflammatory pathways relevant to RA
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Mushrooms provide beta-glucans with immune-modulating properties that may benefit RA, but the high sodium content worsens tissue oedema and the saturated fat load can upregulate TNF-alpha and IL-6 inflammatory cytokines; request preparation with olive oil instead of butter and reduced salt.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Truffle and Mushroom Medley is a rich Western-style dish composed of a sautéed medley of mixed mushrooms finished with black truffle cream, white truffle oil, butter, and parmigiano reggiano cheese. The dish is typically served as a starter or side in upscale restaurant settings, often accompanying pasta, risotto, or grilled meats. The flavour profile is intensely umami and earthy, with the truffle cream and aged parmesan contributing deep savoury notes, while the butter provides a silky, rounded mouthfeel. The texture is tender-firm from the sautéed mushrooms with a lightly creamy coating from the truffle cream sauce. Nutritionally, the dish is energy-dense at 327 kcal per 100 g, driven primarily by its high fat content of 28.5 g/100 g from butter, truffle oil, and cheese. Protein is substantial at 11.2 g/100 g, largely from parmigiano reggiano. Carbohydrate is low at 6.3 g/100 g, making the glycaemic impact minimal (estimated GI ~15, GL ~1.4 per 150 g serving). The primary clinical concern is sodium at 807 mg/100 g, which makes this dish unsuitable for individuals managing hypertension or fluid retention. Saturated fat at approximately 9.8 g/100 g and cholesterol at 105 mg/100 g are also relevant for cardiovascular risk management. Iron content is notably high at 3.94 mg/100 g, contributed by the mushroom varieties. The dish is vegetarian and gluten-free but contains dairy allergens.
common_user_queries: How much sodium is in truffle mushrooms?
Is truffle and mushroom dish high in fat?
What are the calories in truffle mushroom side dish?
Is truffle mushroom suitable for hypertension?

confidence_reason: Core macronutrient values sourced directly from the HPB Foodo canonical record (cr_id G00010963, confidence 0.95, review_status reviewed). Secondary nutrients (saturated fat, sugar, dietary fibre, MUFA, PUFA, trans fat) estimated from USDA SR Legacy component data for the constituent ingredients and are clearly documented as estimates. GI is an analogous estimate from the University of Sydney GI Database for cooked mushrooms, which is the closest approved GI source available.
notes: 1. Food group corrected from 'Grains and staples' to 'Vegetables' and food_subgroup to 'Mushrooms and fungi dishes'. 2. Saturated fat (~9.8 g/100 g), MUFA (~11.2 g/100 g), PUFA (~2.1 g/100 g), trans fat (~0.3 g/100 g), sugar (~1.5 g/100 g), and dietary fibre (~1.2 g/100 g) are estimated from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy component data for cooked mixed mushrooms (USDA #11260), butter (USDA #01001), and parmesan cheese (USDA #01032) weighted by approximate recipe proportions; these are not directly reported by HPB. 3. Fields set to -1 (data not available from HPB or USDA for this composite dish): vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, water_g — detailed micronutrient and moisture data for this specific composite truffle-mushroom preparation are not available in the HPB Foodo database or USDA FoodData Central as a composite entry. 4. GI of 15 is an analogous estimate based on cooked mixed mushrooms from the University of Sydney GI Database (Atkinson et al., Diabetes Care 2021); no direct GI measurement for this specific dish exists in any approved source. 5. Halal status marked false as the dish contains parmigiano reggiano (traditional Italian cheese, animal rennet, not certified halal) and the preparation origin is not halal-certified. 6. This is a Western cuisine dish; multilingual naming requirements apply only to Singaporean, Japanese, Korean, Indonesian, Thai, and Vietnamese dishes per G6 rules — no mandatory local-language names are required for Western cuisine dishes.
